calendar_note: Proleptic Julian; Plutarch and Arrian date to the day after a lunar eclipse astronomically confirmed to 20 September 331 BCE (Julian). Battle date 1 October 331 BCE derived from eclipse chronology.
location_name: Gaugamela plain (near Mosul), Achaemenid Satrapy of Assyria
main_actor: Alexander III of Macedon; Darius III of the Achaemenid Empire
description: Decisive Macedonian victory over the Achaemenid Empire. Alexander's ~47,000 troops defeated Darius III's army (ancient sources claim 250,000, modern estimates 50,000-120,000). Darius fled, was murdered by Bessus the following year. End of Achaemenid Persia; Alexander assumed the Great King's titles.
ethical_notes: Greek sources (Arrian, Diodorus, Curtius, Plutarch) are all retrospective and Hellenocentric. Persian perspective is lost — no Achaemenid source survives describing the battle.

sources.citation: Marsden, E. W. (1964). The Campaign of Gaugamela. Liverpool UP.
Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander III.7-15 (2nd c. CE).

calendar_note: Chinese sources place battle in the seventh month of Tianbao 10 (July-August 751 CE Julian). Arabic sources (al-Tabari) give Dhu'l-Hijjah 133 AH. Exact day not documented.
location_name: Talas River valley (modern Taraz area), Karluk territory
main_actor: Abbasid Caliphate (Ziyad ibn Salih) with Karluk allies; Tang Dynasty (general Gao Xianzhi)
description: Abbasid-Tang clash on the Talas River. The Karluk Turks defected mid-battle, resulting in Tang defeat. Tradition holds that papermaking technology was transmitted westward via captured Chinese artisans, though this is contested by modern scholarship. Marked the furthest western extent of Tang authority in Central Asia.
ethical_notes: The 'papermaking transfer' narrative is a 20th-century interpretive tradition. Earlier Arabic sources (al-Jahiz, al-Tha'alibi) mention Chinese captives but the connection to papermaking transmission is indirect.

sources.citation: Beckwith, C. I. (1987). The Tibetan Empire in Central Asia. Princeton UP, ch. 4.
al-Tabari, Tarikh al-Rusul wa al-Muluk (c. 915 CE).
Zizhi Tongjian 資治通鑑, vol. 216 (1084 CE).

calendar_note: Julian calendar; proleptic Gregorian ~15 June 313. Lactantius records the Licinian rescript published in Nicomedia on 13 June 313.
main_actor: Emperor Constantine I; Emperor Licinius
description: Joint proclamation extending religious tolerance to Christians in the Roman Empire, ending systematic persecution. Property confiscated during the Diocletianic persecution was ordered restored. Preserved in Lactantius's De mortibus persecutorum and Eusebius's Historia Ecclesiastica.
ethical_notes: Modern scholarship (Barnes 1981, Potter 2013) notes the document was not a single 'edict' but a pair of rescripts. The Milan meeting set policy; Licinius's Nicomedia letter (13 June) is the extant text. Constantine's own Christianization was gradual and political.

sources.citation: Lactantius, De mortibus persecutorum 48 (c. 314-315 CE).
Barnes, T. D. (1981). Constantine and Eusebius. Harvard UP.

location_name: Mount Tambora, Sumbawa, Sultanate of Bima, Dutch East Indies
main_actor: Mount Tambora volcano (VEI-7 eruption)
description: Climactic phase of the largest observed volcanic eruption in recorded history. ~38 km3 of magma ejected; ~71,000 immediate and indirect deaths on Sumbawa and Lombok. Stratospheric sulfate aerosols triggered the 'Year Without a Summer' (1816) in the Northern Hemisphere — crop failures, famine in New England, Europe, and Yunnan. Tambora eruption is distinct from the already-cataloged 'Tambora 1815' entry which refers to the climatic effects.
ethical_notes: The Tambora eruption erased the Tambora and Pekat languages and cultures. Dutch colonial records undercount indigenous casualties. The climatic effects disproportionately affected subsistence farmers globally.

sources.citation: Oppenheimer, C. (2003). Climatic, environmental and human consequences of the largest known historic eruption: Tambora volcano (Indonesia) 1815. Progress in Physical Geography 27(2).
Wood, G. D. (2014). Tambora: The Eruption That Changed the World. Princeton UP.

location_name: Lakagígar fissure, southern Iceland (Kingdom of Denmark-Norway)
main_actor: Laki volcanic fissure
description: Eight-month fissure eruption beginning 8 June 1783 released ~14 km3 of basalt lava and vast quantities of SO2. The 'Móðuharðindin' (Mist Hardships) killed ~20% of Iceland's population (~10,000) and 50% of livestock. Sulfate aerosols caused severe crop failures across Europe and North Africa; recent scholarship links Laki to the poor French harvests of 1787-88 contributing to the French Revolution.
ethical_notes: The demographic catastrophe in Iceland was worsened by Danish trade monopoly restrictions preventing emergency grain imports. Reverend Jón Steingrímsson's eyewitness sermon (the 'Fire Sermon' of 20 July 1783) is a unique primary source.

sources.citation: Steingrímsson, J. (1788). Fires of the Earth / Um jarðeldinn í Skaftafellssýslu.
Thordarson, T. & Self, S. (2003). Atmospheric and environmental effects of the 1783-1784 Laki eruption. J. Geophysical Research 108.

name_original: Eruptio Vesuvii 79 — destruction of Pompeii and Herculaneum
calendar_note: Traditional date 24 August 79 CE (Pliny manuscript tradition) is now contested. Archaeological evidence (autumn fruits, heated braziers, wool-making, a charcoal inscription from October) supports 24 October 79 CE (Julian) — accepted by most specialists since 2018. Note: there is already a Vesuvius entry ('eruptio vesuvii') in the DB; this is the corrected/redated entry for the same event with the October 24 date — SKIP.
main_actor: Mount Vesuvius (VEI-5)
description: SKIP — event already present in DB (eruptio vesuvii). Retained here for reference on the date controversy but excluded from new additions.
ethical_notes: SKIP DUPLICATE.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; proleptic Gregorian 7 June 1453. Dated to Tuesday 29 May 1453 by Greek, Latin, and Ottoman sources (857 AH in Ottoman reckoning).
location_name: Constantinople, Byzantine Empire (captured by Ottoman Empire)
main_actor: Sultan Mehmed II 'the Conqueror' of the Ottoman Empire; Emperor Constantine XI Palaiologos (killed)

calendar_note: Julian calendar. Arabic sources (Ibn al-Athir) place the battle on Friday, 27 Dhu'l-Qa'dah 463 AH. SKIP — 'malazgirt muharebesi' already in DB (Turkish name).
main_actor: Sultan Alp Arslan of the Great Seljuk Empire; Emperor Romanos IV Diogenes

ethical_notes: SKIP.

main_actor: Martin Luther; Emperor Charles V; Johann Eck (imperial interrogator)
description: On 17-18 April 1521, Martin Luther appeared before the Imperial Diet at Worms and refused to recant his writings. The phrase 'Hier stehe ich, ich kann nicht anders' ('Here I stand, I can do no other'), though not certainly attested, became emblematic. The Edict of Worms (25 May) declared Luther an outlaw. Triggered the Protestant Reformation's political crystallization.
ethical_notes: Luther's theological courage is celebrated but his later writings (Von den Juden und ihren Lügen, 1543) endorsed violent antisemitism that shaped centuries of persecution in Lutheran territories.

sources.citation: Brecht, M. (1985-1993). Martin Luther (3 vols). Fortress Press.
Acta et res gestae D. Martini Lutheri in Comitiis Principum Wormaciae (1521).

calendar_note: Julian calendar; traditional date 31 October 1517 commemorated as Reformationstag. The physical 'posting' on the Schlosskirche door is debated (Iserloh 1968 argued it was a mailing, not a nailing).
location_name: Wittenberg, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire
main_actor: Martin Luther
description: Luther sent his 'Disputatio pro declaratione virtutis indulgentiarum' (95 Theses) to Archbishop Albrecht of Mainz on 31 October 1517, protesting the sale of indulgences. Traditional account holds he also posted the Latin theses on the Schlosskirche door the same day. Within weeks the text, translated to German and printed, spread across Germany. Conventionally marks the start of the Protestant Reformation.
ethical_notes: The 95 Theses were originally an academic disputation in Latin. Their explosive reception owed much to Tetzel's scandalous indulgence campaign in nearby Brandenburg and to the new printing technology.

sources.citation: Luther, M. (1517). Disputatio pro declaratione virtutis indulgentiarum.
Roper, L. (2016). Martin Luther: Renegade and Prophet. Bodley Head.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; proleptic Gregorian 25 January 1559. Date chosen by John Dee as astrologically auspicious.
main_actor: Queen Elizabeth I; Bishop Owen Oglethorpe of Carlisle (only bishop willing to perform the rite)
description: Elizabeth Tudor crowned at Westminster Abbey after the death of Mary I (17 November 1558). The coronation marked the decisive Protestant turn in England after Mary's Catholic restoration. Most bishops refused to crown her due to religious dispute; Oglethorpe officiated under protest. Elizabeth ruled until 1603 — the longest reign of any English monarch until Victoria.
ethical_notes: The 'Elizabethan Golden Age' framing is partly a Whig historical construction. Contemporary realities included religious persecution of Catholics, the plantation of Ireland, the start of the English slave trade (John Hawkins 1562), and vast rural poverty.

sources.citation: Starkey, D. (2001). Elizabeth: The Struggle for the Throne. HarperCollins.
The Quenes Maiesties Passage (1559).

main_actor: Bohemian Protestant estates (Count Thurn); Imperial Catholic regents Wilhelm Slavata and Jaroslav Martinic (defenestrated)
description: Protestant Bohemian nobles threw two Catholic imperial regents and their secretary from a window of Prague Castle. All three survived (Catholic sources attributed it to divine intervention; Protestant sources to a dung heap). Triggered the Bohemian Revolt and the Thirty Years' War (1618-1648), which devastated Central Europe.
ethical_notes: The defenestration consciously echoed the First Defenestration of Prague (1419), which began the Hussite Wars. Czech historiography frames both as acts of religious freedom; German Catholic historiography as sedition.

sources.citation: Wilson, P. H. (2009). Europe's Tragedy: A History of the Thirty Years War. Allen Lane.

calendar_note: Gregorian calendar. SKIP — 'prise de la bastille' already in DB.
main_actor: Parisian crowd; Marquis de Launay (governor, killed); Hôtel des Invalides
description: SKIP DUPLICATE.
ethical_notes: SKIP.

calendar_note: Gregorian calendar. In the French Revolutionary calendar: 2 Pluviôse An I.
location_name: Place de la Révolution (Place de la Concorde), Paris, French Republic
main_actor: King Louis XVI; Charles-Henri Sanson (executioner); National Convention
description: King Louis XVI was guillotined at 10:22 on 21 January 1793 after being convicted of treason by the National Convention (387 to 334 for execution). His death inaugurated the French First Republic as a regicidal polity and triggered the War of the First Coalition. Marie Antoinette followed on 16 October 1793.
ethical_notes: The vote on execution was narrow and contested. The execution radicalized European monarchs against the Revolution, hardened royalist resistance in the Vendée, and provided precedent for later political executions.

sources.citation: Hardman, J. (2016). The Life of Louis XVI. Yale UP.
Procès-verbal de la Convention nationale, 21 janvier 1793.

location_name: Waterloo, United Kingdom of the Netherlands (modern Belgium)
main_actor: Duke of Wellington (Anglo-allied); Gebhard von Blücher (Prussian); Napoleon Bonaparte
description: Napoleon's final defeat. ~72,000 French faced ~68,000 Anglo-allied troops under Wellington, reinforced in the afternoon by ~50,000 Prussians under Blücher. ~47,000 total casualties over ~10 hours. Napoleon abdicated 22 June and was exiled to Saint Helena. Ended the Hundred Days and the Napoleonic Wars; inaugurated the Concert of Europe and ~40 years of major-power peace.
ethical_notes: British memory celebrates Wellington; Prussian memory emphasizes Blücher's decisive arrival. French memory casts it as heroic tragedy. All three framings elide the ~200,000 Dutch-Belgian civilians affected by the campaign.

sources.citation: Roberts, A. (2005). Waterloo: June 18, 1815, the Battle for Modern Europe. HarperCollins.

name_original: Battle of Austerlitz (Battle of the Three Emperors)
location_name: Austerlitz (Slavkov u Brna), Moravia, Austrian Empire
main_actor: Emperor Napoleon I; Emperor Francis II of Austria; Tsar Alexander I of Russia
description: Napoleon's tactical masterpiece. ~65,000 French defeated ~85,000 Austro-Russian troops on the first anniversary of Napoleon's coronation. ~27,000 Allied casualties vs ~9,000 French. Led to the Peace of Pressburg (26 Dec 1805), dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire (1806), and establishment of the Confederation of the Rhine.
ethical_notes: Austerlitz is often cited as the apex of Napoleonic warfare, but its strategic consequences — the Confederation of the Rhine and the German reorganization — laid groundwork for the 19th-century German national question.

sources.citation: Chandler, D. G. (1966). The Campaigns of Napoleon. Macmillan.

main_actor: Klemens von Metternich (Austria); Viscount Castlereagh (Britain); Prince Hardenberg (Prussia); Tsar Alexander I (Russia); Charles-Maurice de Talleyrand (France)
description: The Final Act of the Congress of Vienna (9 June 1815) reorganized Europe after Napoleon's defeat. 216 articles redrew borders: German Confederation created (39 states), Poland partitioned again, Dutch Netherlands united with Belgium, Norway joined with Sweden. Established the 'Concert of Europe' great-power mechanism and the principle of collective intervention against revolution.
ethical_notes: The Congress restored dynastic legitimacy at the expense of national self-determination — Poles, Italians, Belgians, and Germans lived under imposed arrangements. The conservative order held until 1848 and, in modified form, until 1914.

sources.citation: Zamoyski, A. (2007). Rites of Peace: The Fall of Napoleon and the Congress of Vienna. HarperPress.

main_actor: Georges Clemenceau, Woodrow Wilson, David Lloyd George, Vittorio Orlando; German delegation (Hermann Müller, Johannes Bell)

calendar_note: Julian calendar; traditionally dated 10 October 732 (Mozarabic Chronicle). Some sources prefer October 25. SKIP — 'bellum pictaviense' already in DB.
location_name: Between Poitiers and Tours, Kingdom of the Franks
main_actor: Charles Martel; Abd al-Rahman ibn Abd Allah al-Ghafiqi (killed)

ethical_notes: SKIP.

main_actor: Napoleon Bonaparte; Pope Pius VII; Empress Josephine
description: Napoleon I crowned Emperor of the French at Notre-Dame. Pope Pius VII attended but Napoleon famously placed the crown on his own head, signalling that his authority was not derived from the Church. Ended the French Consulate; established the Premier Empire. Commemorated by David's painting Le Sacre (1807).
ethical_notes: The coronation combined Carolingian symbolism (hence the Pope) with revolutionary self-assertion (the self-crowning gesture). Its staging consciously broke with the Bourbon Rheims tradition.

sources.citation: Englund, S. (2004). Napoleon: A Political Life. Scribner.

name_original: Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen
calendar_note: Gregorian calendar. SKIP — 'déclaration des droits de l'homme et du citoyen' already in DB.
main_actor: National Constituent Assembly
description: SKIP DUPLICATE.
ethical_notes: SKIP.

main_actor: Continental Congress; Thomas Jefferson (principal author); John Hancock (President of Congress)
description: The Second Continental Congress adopted the Declaration of Independence from Great Britain on 4 July 1776. Drafting committee: Jefferson, Adams, Franklin, Sherman, Livingston. Independence was legally voted on 2 July; the written text was adopted 4 July; most delegates signed the engrossed copy on 2 August. Founding text of the United States.
ethical_notes: The 'all men are created equal' clause excluded enslaved Black people, Indigenous peoples, and women from political rights. Jefferson himself held over 600 people in slavery across his lifetime. The Declaration's grievance list included incitement of 'merciless Indian Savages', a clause Indigenous scholars identify as foundational to dispossession.

sources.citation: Maier, P. (1997). American Scripture: Making the Declaration of Independence. Knopf.

main_actor: Constitutional Convention; George Washington (presiding); James Madison (principal architect)
description: 39 of 55 delegates signed the US Constitution on 17 September 1787, concluding a convention that began 25 May. Replaced the Articles of Confederation. Ratified by the ninth state (New Hampshire) on 21 June 1788; effective 4 March 1789. Commemorated as Constitution Day / Citizenship Day.
ethical_notes: The Constitution included the Three-Fifths Compromise (Article I §2), counting enslaved persons as 3/5 for congressional apportionment, and guaranteed the slave trade until 1808 (Article I §9). Indigenous nations were excluded from constitutional citizenship until 1924.

sources.citation: Beeman, R. (2009). Plain, Honest Men: The Making of the American Constitution. Random House.

main_actor: George Washington; Comte de Rochambeau (French); Lord Cornwallis (British, surrendered)
description: SKIP — 'surrender at yorktown' already in DB.
ethical_notes: SKIP.

name_original: Abolition of slavery in British Empire (Slavery Abolition Act effective)
location_name: British Empire (legislated in Westminster; applied throughout colonies)
main_actor: UK Parliament; William Wilberforce (posthumous, died July 1833); enslaved peoples of Caribbean, Mauritius, Cape Colony
description: The Slavery Abolition Act 1833 took effect on 1 August 1834, formally ending slavery across most of the British Empire. ~800,000 enslaved people were re-categorized as 'apprentices' (forced to work unpaid for their former owners for 4-6 more years; this apprenticeship ended 1 August 1838). £20 million compensation was paid to slave-owners (~40% of the Treasury's annual revenue); enslaved people received no compensation. Territories under East India Company rule were exempted until 1843.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: 'abolition' narratives in British memory centre Wilberforce and Parliament while erasing Caribbean revolts (Haitian Revolution 1791, Bussa's Rebellion Barbados 1816, Demerara 1823, Baptist War Jamaica 1831-32) that made maintaining slavery economically untenable. The £20M loan to compensate slave-owners was only paid off by UK taxpayers in 2015.

sources.citation: Drescher, S. (2009). Abolition: A History of Slavery and Antislavery. Cambridge UP.

main_actor: President Abraham Lincoln
description: Lincoln's Emancipation Proclamation, issued 22 September 1862, took effect on 1 January 1863. It declared enslaved people in Confederate-held areas free — ~3.5 million people. It did not apply to border slave states loyal to the Union (Delaware, Maryland, Kentucky, Missouri, West Virginia) — those areas' ~400,000 enslaved people waited for the Thirteenth Amendment (ratified 6 December 1865). Transformed the Civil War's aims from preservation of Union to abolition.
ethical_notes: The Proclamation's practical effect depended on Union military advance. Texas slaves learned of emancipation only on 19 June 1865 (commemorated as Juneteenth). Lincoln's earlier colonization proposals (to remove Black Americans to Liberia or Central America) are often omitted from heroic narratives.

sources.citation: Foner, E. (2010). The Fiery Trial: Abraham Lincoln and American Slavery. Norton.

main_actor: Gavrilo Princip (Young Bosnia); Archduke Franz Ferdinand and Sophie Chotek (killed)
description: On the morning of 28 June 1914, Gavrilo Princip, a 19-year-old Bosnian Serb member of Young Bosnia, shot and killed Archduke Franz Ferdinand, heir presumptive to the Austro-Hungarian throne, and his wife Sophie, on Sarajevo's Latiner Bridge. The date was Vidovdan, a Serb national holiday marking the 1389 Battle of Kosovo. Triggered the July Crisis and, one month later, the outbreak of World War I.
ethical_notes: The Austro-Hungarian response (ultimatum to Serbia 23 July 1914) reflected pre-existing war aims; the assassination provided occasion. The Black Hand's involvement (Apis, Serbian military intelligence) implicated elements of the Serbian state, though the government's role is contested.

sources.citation: Dedijer, V. (1966). The Road to Sarajevo. Simon & Schuster.
Clark, C. (2012). The Sleepwalkers.

main_actor: Georgy Zhukov, Vasily Chuikov (Red Army); Friedrich Paulus (surrendered, 6th Army)
description: On 2 February 1943, the remnants of the German 6th Army surrendered in Stalingrad, ending the five-month battle (17 July 1942 – 2 February 1943). ~91,000 Germans were taken prisoner; of these only ~5,000-6,000 survived Soviet captivity. Total casualties (military and civilian) estimated ~2 million. Considered the strategic turning point of the Eastern Front and, arguably, WWII in Europe.
ethical_notes: Stalingrad's Soviet civilian population (~500,000 pre-battle) suffered catastrophic losses — Stalin's 19 August 1942 refusal to evacuate civilians, meant to steel the defense, condemned many. Both Nazi and Soviet sides committed atrocities (summary executions of deserters; ethnic Germans deported).

sources.citation: Beevor, A. (1998). Stalingrad. Viking.
